articles.title: Who has profited most from the war on Iran?
articles.summary: Defence contractors, energy companies and investment banks saw profits soar as war and uncertainty upended markets.
articles.titleZh: 谁从伊朗战争中获益最多？
articles.summaryZh: 随着战争和不确定性颠覆市场，国防承包商、能源公司和投资银行的利润飙升。

contentParagraphs.en: Nearly four months since the United States and Israel first launched strikes on Iran, sending energy prices soaring and disrupting global trade, the two sides are holding talks in Switzerland with a memorandum of understanding in place establishing a 60-day ceasefire and framework for negotiations on Iran’s nuclear programme, sanctions relief and the future of the Strait of Hormuz.A lasting deal could ease the economic pain felt by businesses and consumers worldwide. But, for some companies, the conflict has proven immensely profitable.Defence contractors, oil and gas producers and investment banks are among the sectors that have seen profits soar as war and uncertainty have upended global markets.So, who exactly has profited the most?Energy firmsIn terms of hard, cold dollars, no one sector has benefitted more directly from the war than energy. Before the war, about one-fifth of the world’s oil and liquified natural gas (LNG) passed through the Strait of Hormuz.Disruptions to shipping through the narrow waterway sent crude prices soaring and triggered sharp swings in global energy markets.At one point, Brent crude briefly touched $126 a barrel, its highest price in four years. The price has since dropped to pre-war levels of about $72 per barrel.Higher prices translated into a massive cash flow windfall for some oil producers who were also able to benefit from larger price gaps between regional energy markets.Saudi Aramco’s first-quarter profits rose by 25 percent to $32.5bn compared with the same period a year earlier. The firm leveraged its 1,200km East-West pipeline to the Red Sea, bypassing the Strait of Hormuz, to maintain exports at a capacity of seven million barrels per day, while selling oil at higher prices.British Petroleum (BP) reported first-quarter profits of $3.2bn – more than double the previous year and roundly beating analyst expectations of $2.67bn.Following regional strikes on Qatar’s Ras Laffan facility, Shell’s co-owned Pearl GTL – a gas-to-liquids plant converting raw natural gas into liquid fuels – saw its Train 2 processing unit sustain severe damage. Shell estimated repairs will take a year to complete. Despite this, the group maintained a strong balance sheet, reporting profits of $6.9bn compared with about $5.6bn in the previous year’s first quarter.Despite seeing 15 percent of its global production shut down across Qatar, Iraq and the United Arab Emirates, TotalEnergies reported adjusted net income of $5.4bn compared with $4.2bn in the same quarter a year prior. It maintained 210,000 barrels per day of onshore UAE production by routing exports through the Fujairah Terminal, bypassing the Strait of Hormuz.Rystad Energy, an independent energy research firm, analysed the cash flow of major oil companies in April, at the height of market volatility, comparing returns at under $65 per barrel pre-war against $100 per barrel during the war, and found that Saudi Aramco stood to gain the most from higher prices.Thomas Liles, senior vice president of Upstream Research at Rystad Energy, told Al Jazeera: “Each one of those players is going to end up in a net positive place if we see higher prices persist throughout the year. It’s really just a question of how much of that cash flow they end up capturing.”The beneficiaries extend beyond oil majors. With about one-fifth of global LNG supplies normally being shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, US LNG firms such as Venture Global and Cheniere Energy are well positioned to gain as buyers seek more secure supplies.“I would say probably most companies without a very concentrated Middle East or, let’s say, west of Hormuz exposure stand to benefit. Some of that is going to include US shale oil players, Canadian oil sands players, the IOCs [International Oil Companies], producers in Latin America, LNG players like Venture Global who sell more into the spot market, so there are a number of winners here for different reasons,” Liles told Al Jazeera.But analysts caution the windfall may be short-lived – a tentative US-Iran ceasefire has already pushed prices lower, and prolonged high energy costs risk weakening demand and tipping economies towards recession.Defence contractorsWithin days of the first US-Israel strikes on Iran at the end of February, the heads of the world’s largest arms manufacturers met at the White House and agreed to ramp up weapons production as US munitions stockpiles dwindled.Executives from RTX, Lockheed Martin, Boeing, Northrop Grumman, BAE Systems, L3Harris and Honeywell attended the talks. All are sitting on billions of dollars in orders, backlogs that are likely to grow as governments rush to replenish their arsenals.Only weeks before the conflict, US President Donald Trump approved a $500bn increase in defence funding sought by Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth. On March 19, Hegseth defended a further request of $200bn in additional funding from Congress by telling reporters, “It takes money to kill bad guys.”Investors have already begun betting on a prolonged boom. The strongest performances came from Boeing, RTX, L3Harris and Northrop Grumman, all of which reported solid revenue growth and either raised or reaffirmed their full-year guidance.Boeing’s revenue climbed 14 percent to $22.2bn in the first three months of the year from higher aircraft deliveries. Though the company remained loss-making, it significantly narrowed its net loss to $7m from $31m in the same period a year earlier. Meanwhile, Northrop Grumman’s orders backlog hit a record $95.6bn, boosted by the addition of classified programmes and F-35-related work.The war is reinforcing an already lucrative model, experts say. US defence contracts account for a substantial share of revenues for munitions producers. Between 2020 and 2024, private firms received $2.4 trillion in Pentagon contracts – more than half of the department’s discretionary spending, according to the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft and the Costs of War projec
contentParagraphs.zh: 自美国和以色列首次对伊朗发动打击、导致能源价格飙升并扰乱全球贸易以来，近四个月以来，两国正在瑞士举行会谈，签署了一份谅解备忘录，确定了为期 60 天的停火以及就伊朗核计划、解除制裁和霍尔木兹海峡的未来进行谈判的框架。一项持久的协议可以缓解全球企业和消费者所感受到的经济痛苦。但是，对于一些公司来说，这场冲突已经证明了巨大的利润。随着战争和不确定性颠覆了全球市场，国防承包商、石油和天然气生产商以及投资银行都是利润飙升的行业。那么，到底谁获利最多呢？能源公司就硬美元而言，没有哪个行业比能源行业更直接地从战争中受益。战前，全球约五分之一的石油和液化天然气（LNG）经过霍尔木兹海峡。狭窄水道运输中断导致原油价格飙升，引发全球能源市场剧烈波动。布伦特原油一度一度触及每桶126美元，创四年来最高价。此后价格已降至每桶约 72 美元的战前水平。价格上涨为一些石油生产商带来了巨额现金流，他们也能够从区域能源市场之间更大的价差中受益。沙特阿美第一季度利润较上年同期增长 25%，达到 325 亿美元。该公司利用其 1,200 公里的东西走向红海的管道，绕过霍尔木兹海峡，维持每天 700 万桶的出口能力，同时以更高的价格出售石油。英国石油公司 (BP) 公布第一季度利润为 32 亿美元，比上年同期增加一倍以上，全面超过分析师预期的 26.7 亿美元。在卡塔尔拉斯拉凡设施发生区域性罢工后，壳牌的共同拥有的 Pearl GTL（一家将原天然气转化为液体燃料的天然气制油工厂）的 2 号列车处理装置遭受严重损坏。壳牌预计修复工作需要一年时间才能完成。尽管如此，该集团仍保持强劲的资产负债表，报告利润为 69 亿美元，而去年第一季度约为 56 亿美元。 尽管卡塔尔、伊拉克和阿拉伯联合酋长国全球 15% 的生产被关闭，TotalEnergies 报告调整后净利润为 54 亿美元，而去年同期为 42 亿美元。通过绕过霍尔木兹海峡，通过富查伊拉码头进行出口，维持了阿联酋陆上日产量 21 万桶。独立能源研究公司 Rystad Energy 分析了 4 月份市场波动最严重时主要石油公司的现金流，将战前每桶低于 65 美元的回报率与战时每桶 100 美元的回报率进行了比较，发现沙特阿美公司将从更高的价格中获得最大收益。 高级副总裁托马斯·莱尔斯 (Thomas Liles) Rystad Energy上游研究总裁告诉半岛电视台：“如果我们看到全年价格持续上涨，那么这些参与者中的每一个最终都将处于净利好位置。这实际上只是他们最终获得多少现金流的问题。”受益者不仅限于石油巨头。全球约五分之一的液化天然气供应通常通过霍尔木兹海峡运输，随着买家寻求更安全的供应，Venture Global 和 Cheniere Energy 等美国液化天然气公司处于有利地位。莱尔斯告诉半岛电视台：“像 Venture Global 一样，它们在现货市场上出售更多产品，因此这里有许多赢家。”但分析师警告称，这笔意外之财可能是短暂的——美伊暂时停火已经压低了价格，长期居高不下的能源成本可能会削弱需求并导致经济陷入衰退。 国防承包商 2 月底，美国和以色列首次对伊朗发动空袭，几天后，全球最大的军火制造商的负责人在白宫举行了会面。并同意随着美国弹药库存的减少而增加武器生产。RTX、洛克希德·马丁、波音、诺斯罗普·格鲁曼、BAE Systems、L3Harris 和霍尼韦尔的高管参加了会谈。所有这些都坐拥数十亿美元的订单，随着各国政府急于补充军火库，积压的订单可能会增加。就在冲突发生前几周，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普批准了国防部长皮特·赫格斯所寻求的增加 5000 亿美元的国防资金。 3 月 19 日，赫格赛斯为国会进一步提出的 2000 亿美元额外资金请求进行了辩护，他告诉记者，“杀死坏人需要钱。”投资者已经开始押注长期繁荣。表现最强劲的是波音、RTX、L3Harris 和诺斯罗普·格鲁曼，它们均报告了稳健的收入增长，并上调或重申了全年指引。由于飞机交付量增加，波音今年前三个月的收入增长了 14%，达到 222 亿美元。尽管该公司仍处于亏损状态，但净亏损从去年同期的 3100 万美元大幅收窄至 700 万美元。与此同时，由于机密项目和 F-35 相关工作的增加，诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司的积压订单达到创纪录的 956 亿美元。专家表示，这场战争正在强化本已利润丰厚的模式。美国国防合同占军火生产商收入的很大一部分。根据昆西负责任治国与战争成本研究所的数据，2020 年至 2024 年间，私营企业在五角大楼获得了 2.4 万亿美元的合同，占该部门可自由支配支出的一半以上

articles.title: The abundant but expensive energy source that's under your feet
articles.summary: Start-ups are taking fresh approaches to geothermal energy, but will the economics work?
articles.titleZh: 你脚下丰富但昂贵的能源
articles.summaryZh: 初创企业正在采取新的地热能方法，但经济有效吗？

contentParagraphs.en: It's hard to get Democrat and Republican politicians to agree on much at the moment, but the benefits of geothermal energy is one rare area of consensus. Geothermal energy makes use of natural heat below the Earth's surface and the next generation of technology can access hotter, deeper and more varied locations than ever before.Broadly, the low greenhouse gas emissions of geothermal plants appeals to liberals, while conservatives like the additional energy independence of geothermal, plus the use of drilling technology familiar in the oil and gas industry. Some US states are trying to accelerate permits for geothermal plants and in April senators from both parties introduced the Next-Generation Geothermal, external Research and Development Act.The legislation would direct the Department of Energy to support the development and commercialisation of the next generation of geothermal energy systems.One emerging type is known as enhanced geothermal systems (EGS).In EGS, underground rock is fractured hydraulically. That's done by pumping pressurised fluid into one well, and then collecting steam or hot water from another well.Better known as fracking, this technique has become well known and controversial (particularly in the UK) in the oil and gas industry."It's the same techniques and up to a point it's the same industry as well," sums up Gernot Wagner, a climate economist at Columbia Business School in New York. But "from a climate perspective, there's a huge difference," he adds.For him, the risk of seismic activity, by creating cracks underground, is outweighed by the benefits of an energy source that is renewable, always-on and large-capacity."Based on where we are, moving much faster, much bigger in the direction of using much more geothermal, frankly, is all good news," Wagner says.To go faster and deeper will require advances in drilling technologies.Companies are developing drilling equipment that is more stable when breaking through hard rock at high temperatures.Some firms are even aiming to penetrate rock without using standard drills. Quaise, a company with roots at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), is using a technology called millimetre wave drilling. The frequency is similar to that of microwaves.Quaise's application involves "sending electromagnetic waves in the microwave millimetre wave spectrum to essentially melt and vaporise through the rock," explains Harry Kelso, Quaise's communications manager.Traditional geothermal energy clusters around hotspots on the earth's surface where very hot rocks can be easily accessed. "Millimetre wave drilling really enables you to access super-hot geothermal just about anywhere in the world," says Kelso. While Quaise is planning to use some conventional drilling at the project site it's developing in Oregon, Kelso says that conventional drills start to break down more quickly when it reaches very hard rock.Replacing drill bits increases the cost and time of drilling.In Quaise's case, Kelso says, "millimetre wave drilling is really what changes that because we're not using a physical drill bit."Other companies are also working on advanced drilling technology, such as projectiles that move several times faster than the speed of sound.Another crucial resource in the process is water. While some types of next-generation geothermal could create risks of water contamination or overconsumption, careful design can avoid this problem.Initially Quaise's system requires a lot of water, but according to Kelso, once the water is in the system it is continually circulated over the super-hot rocks."We're essentially just recycling the water over and over," he says.Quaise is continuing to raise funds, with the aim of its Oregon project being up and running by 2030.Like other early versions of geothermal systems, it's an expensive project to get up and running."The economics are somewhat challenging," Kelso admits. "Geothermal today is still more expensive because you are not getting as much power out of the well as you would if you were using that well for fossil fuel."But Quaise hopes that by targeting very high temperatures, of between 300C and 500C, the economics will improve.While the higher end of that temperature range is ambitious, it's a case of the-hotter-the-better."It allows you to get 10 times more energy per well from geothermal, which changes the economics and the power potential of geothermal," according to Kelso.In May, the Texas company Fervo Energy generated huge interest by becoming the first next-generation geothermal company to become publicly traded. It was initially valued at around $7.7bn.At the time of writing, shares are up around 18% from their IPO price.Fervo quotes a construction cost for its Utah plant of $7,000 per kilowatt of electricity, which it says is comparable to traditional nuclear power, external.And while that's expensive, Fervo points out that, like other renewable energy sources, it does not have any ongoing costs for fuel."Over time, our goal is to achieve scale and drive down prices such that we're able to outcompete gas," the company said in its IPO filing.Fervo has one high-profile customer - in 2021 it signed a deal, external sell its energy to Google, which needs vast amounts of electricity of its new datacentres.It also has backing from Breakthrough Energy, a venture by Microsoft founder Bill Gates , externalto accelerate innovative electricity production. Such investment is badly needed for next-gen geothermal firms, which have enormous capital costs. Datacentre projects alone won't be enough to move the needle, according to the International Energy Agency.Both customer demand and costs remain uncertain. The climate solutions organisation Project Drawdown, external notes that "early projects carry a significant risk of cost overruns".Nevertheless Columbia researcher Wagner believes geothermal has tremendous potential and is not just hype.He emphasises that commodities like oil, gas and c
contentParagraphs.zh: 目前很难让民主党和共和党政客达成很多共识，但地热能的好处是一个罕见的共识领域。地热能利用地表以下的自然热量，下一代技术可以比以往更热、更深、更多样化的地点。总的来说，地热发电厂的低温室气体排放吸引了自由派，而保守派则喜欢地热的额外能源独立性，以及使用石油和天然气行业熟悉的钻井技术。美国一些州正试图加快地热发电厂的许可进程，四月份两党参议员提出了《下一代地热外部研究与开发法案》。该立法将指示能源部支持下一代地热能系统的开发和商业化。一种新兴类型被称为增强型地热系统 (EGS)。在 EGS 中，地下岩石通过水力破裂。这是通过将加压流体泵入一口井，然后从另一口井收集蒸汽或热水来完成的。这种技术更广为人知的名称是水力压裂，它在石油和天然气行业中已广为人知且颇具争议（尤其是在英国）。“这是相同的技术，在某种程度上也是同一个行业，”纽约哥伦比亚商学院的气候经济学家格诺特·瓦格纳总结道。但他补充道，“从气候角度来看，两者之间存在巨大差异。”对他来说，可再生、永远在线和大容量能源的好处超过了地震活动在地下产生裂缝的风险。瓦格纳说：“坦率地说，就我们目前的情况而言，在使用更多地热的方向上进展得更快、更大，这都是好消息。”要更快、更深，就需要钻探技术的进步。各公司正在开发钻探设备，在高温下穿透坚硬的岩石时更加稳定。一些公司甚至希望在不使用标准钻头的情况下穿透岩石。 Quaise 是一家源自麻省理工学院 (MIT) 的公司，正在使用一种称为毫米波钻孔的技术。该频率与微波的频率相似。Quaise 的通信经理 Harry Kelso 解释说，Quaise 的应用涉及“发送微波毫米波频谱中的电磁波，使岩石基本上熔化和蒸发”。传统的地热能聚集在地球表面的热点周围，在那里可以轻松获取非常热的岩石。 “毫米波钻探确实使您能够在世界任何地方获得超热地热，”凯尔索说。虽然 Quaise 计划在俄勒冈州开发的项目现场使用一些传统钻探，但 Kelso 表示，传统钻头在到达非常坚硬的岩石时开始损坏得更快。更换钻头会增加钻探的成本和时间。在 Quaise 的案例中，Kelso 说，“毫米波钻探确实改变了这一情况，因为我们没有使用物理钻头。”其他公司也在研究先进的钻探技术，例如移动速度比速度快几倍的射弹该过程中的另一个重要资源是水。虽然某些类型的下一代地热可能会造成水污染或过度消耗的风险，但仔细的设计可以避免这个问题。最初，Quaise 的系统需要大量的水，但根据凯尔索的说法，一旦水进入系统，它就会在超热的岩石上不断循环。他说：“我们基本上只是一遍又一遍地循环利用水。”Quaise 正在继续筹集资金，目标是在 2030 年俄勒冈州项目启动并运行。与其他早期项目一样地热系统的版本，启动和运行是一个昂贵的项目。“经济上有些挑战，”凯尔索承认。 “今天的地热发电仍然更加昂贵，因为你从井中获得的电力不如使用化石燃料时那么多。”但奎斯希望，通过瞄准 300 摄氏度到 500 摄氏度之间的非常高的温度，经济性将会提高。虽然这个温度范围的上限是雄心勃勃的，但这是一个越热越好的情况。“它允许你从地热中获得 10 倍多的能量，这改变了经济性凯尔索表示。今年 5 月，德克萨斯州 Fervo Energy 公司成为第一家上市的下一代地热公司，引起了巨大的兴趣。它最初的估值约为 77 亿美元。截至撰写本文时，股价较 IPO 价格上涨约 18%。Fervo 给出的犹他州工厂建设成本为每千瓦 7,000 美元，据称这与传统的外部核电相当。Fervo 指出，虽然成本昂贵，但与其他可再生能源一样，它没有任何持续的燃料成本。“随着时间的推移，我们的目标是实现规模化并降低价格该公司在 IPO 文件中表示，我们能够在竞争中击败天然气。Fervo 有一个备受瞩目的客户 - 2021 年，它签署了一项协议，将其能源外部出售给谷歌，而谷歌的新数据中心需要大量电力。它还得到了微软创始人比尔·盖茨 (Bill Gates) 的合资企业 Breakthrough Energy 的支持，以加速创新电力生产。对于资本成本巨大的下一代地热公司来说，急需此类投资。国际能源署表示，仅靠数据中心项目还不足以起到推动作用。客户需求和成本仍然不确定。气候解决方案组织 Project Drawdown 外部指出，“早期项目存在成本超支的重大风险”。尽管如此，哥伦比亚大学研究员瓦格纳认为地热具有巨大潜力，而不仅仅是炒作。他强调，石油、天然气和煤炭等大宗商品

articles.title: Who could be the UK's next chancellor?
articles.summary: The prime minister's resignation has fired the starting gun on the race to be in charge of the UK's finances.
articles.titleZh: 谁可能成为英国下一任财政大臣？
articles.summaryZh: 首相的辞职打响了英国财政主管竞赛的发令枪。

contentParagraphs.en: Miliband is now the bookmakers' strong favourite for the number two job in British politics, with the former Labour party leader politically closer to Burnham than other rivals.Paul Johnson, former director of think-tank the Institute for Fiscal Studies, sees this closeness as a positive."You really don't want people in Number 10 and Number 11 having very different views," he says.However, opinions differ on whether former Treasury adviser Miliband would receive the backing of the financial markets, which the government depends on to lend money.Nick Macpherson, the former permanent secretary at the Treasury, told the Financial Times: "The key to gaining the confidence of the markets is to articulate, implement and deliver a coherent strategy. "Miliband is one of the few cabinet members with the intellect, experience, and authority to do that."Yet, others see Miliband as an inflation risk, believing his drive for net zero as energy secretary as partly responsible for the UK's high energy prices compared to other countries.Analysts say that reputation, whether accurate or not, could affect how bond markets react to his time as chancellor.Lord Richard Walker, the boss of Iceland and the government's cost-of-living tsar, has warned Miliband would be "a disaster" in the role.He said Miliband had been "far too ideological" about tackling climate change, and that his policies were "putting unfair pressure on households... in a very regressive way".The head of the Unite union, Sharon Graham says Miliband as chancellor would be a "noose around the neck" of job creation, external because of his opposition to new oil and gas drilling in the North Sea.However, the TSSA union backs Miliband, with the Labour-affiliated rail union saying he would be willing to take a "different approach" to "delivering an economy that works for everyone".Wes Streeting, a former contender for the Labour leadership, was the early favourite for chancellor, with suggestions that he could be awarded the job for coming out and backing Burnham and withdrawing his own ambitions.However, economist and cross-bench peer Lord Jim O'Neill, who has been providing advice to Burnham, has warned against this approach.While not naming any specific person, he told the BBC: "There are clearly some people pushing to be chancellor who feel they are owed it for their support."Lord O'Neill says the advice he has given Burnham is to "figure out what his priorities are as prime minister before he picks a chancellor".Though Burnham may appreciate Streeting's backing, the pair's politics differ - with Burnham seen to be inclined to spend more than Streeting.Simon French, chief economist at consultancy Panmure Liberum, says Streeting is a "relatively market-friendly option" because of his pro-growth comments, but also a political risk because he might someday want to be prime minister.As for the idea that Streeting could get the job because of his support rather than his abilities, French said: "Politics is what politics is. It's a popularity contest."Then there are the longlist of wildcards.One of those is current Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood, who is reported to be financially conservative but has limited economic experience.Former defence secretary John Healey, who very publicly quit because he did not believe the government was spending enough on defence, is another option.However, Paul Johnson says Burnham would essentially be committing to meeting that spending demand if he chose him. "If I was Andy Burnham, I would not want to tie myself to that particular pillar that quickly," he said.Bookmakers and reports also mention chief secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones, who ruled himself out of the running for the leadership, and former chief executive of the Resolution Foundation Torsten Bell as outsiders.While some politicians are more likely to be hired than others, each one will want the job.As Lord O'Neil puts it: "The ones whose names are in the papers are the ones who are putting themselves forward."
contentParagraphs.zh: 米利班德现在是博彩公司最青睐的英国政坛第二号人物，这位前工党领袖在政治上比其他竞争对手更接近伯纳姆。智囊团财政研究所前所长保罗·约翰逊认为这种密切关系是积极的。“你真的不希望第10位和第11位的人有截然不同的观点，”他说。然而，对于前财政部顾问米利班德是否会得到金融市场的支持，人们的意见不一。财政部前常任秘书长尼克·麦克弗森 (Nick Macpherson) 向英国《金融时报》表示：“获得市场信心的关键是阐明、实施和交付连贯的战略。米利班德是少数几个有智慧、经验和权威做到这一点的内阁成员之一。”然而，其他人则将米利班德视为通胀风险，认为他作为能源大臣推动的净零排放是英国能源价格高于其他国家的部分原因。分析师表示，声誉，无论准确与否，都可能影响债券市场对他担任总理期间的反应。冰岛老板、政府生活成本沙皇理查德·沃克勋爵警告说，米利班德在这个职位上将是“一场灾难”。他说，米利班德在应对气候变化问题上“过于意识形态化”，他的政策“以一种非常倒退的方式给家庭带来不公平的压力……”。联合工会主席莎朗·格雷厄姆表示米利班德担任财政大臣将成为创造就业机会的“绞索”，因为他反对在北海进行新的石油和天然气钻探。然而，TSSA工会支持米利班德，工党附属铁路工会表示，他愿意采取“不同的方法”来“提供一个适合所有人的经济”。前工党领导层竞争者韦斯·斯特里廷是财政大臣的早期热门人选，有人建议他可以获得这份工作。然而，一直向伯纳姆提供建议的经济学家兼中立议员吉姆·奥尼尔勋爵对这种做法提出了警告。虽然他没有透露任何具体人选，但他告诉英国广播公司：“显然有一些力争成为财政大臣的人认为他们应该得到支持。”奥尼尔勋爵表示，他给伯纳姆的建议是“在选择总理之前弄清楚他作为总理的优先事项是什么”尽管伯纳姆可能会欣赏斯特里廷的支持，但两人的政治立场不同——伯纳姆似乎倾向于比斯特里廷花更多的钱。咨询公司Panmure Liberum的首席经济学家西蒙·弗伦奇(Simon French)表示，斯特里廷是一个“相对对市场友好的选择”，因为他发表了支持经济增长的言论，但也存在政治风险，因为他有一天可能想成为总理。至于斯特里廷能得到这份工作是因为他的支持而不是他的能力，弗伦奇表示： “政治就是政治。这是一场人气竞赛。“然后还有一长串的通配符。其中之一是现任内政大臣沙巴纳·马哈茂德，据报道他在财务上保守，但经济经验有限。前国防部长约翰·希利是另一种选择，他因不相信政府在国防上投入足够而公开辞职。然而，保罗·约翰逊表示，如果伯纳姆选择他，他基本上将致力于满足支出需求。“如果我是安迪·伯纳姆，我不想打平他说。博彩公司和报道还提到了总理首席秘书达伦·琼斯（Darren Jones）和决议基金会前首席执行官托斯顿·贝尔（Torsten Bell），前者将自己排除在竞选领导层之外，而决议基金会前首席执行官托斯顿·贝尔（Torsten Bell）则被视为局外人。虽然有些政客比其他人更有可能被聘用，但每个人都想要这份工作。正如奥尼尔勋爵所说：“名字出现在报纸上的人就是那些提出自己意见的人。”

articles.title: What made the deadly Venezuelan earthquakes different
articles.summary: It appears the two big earthquakes in Venezuela that occurred in rapid succession may have involved two separate fault lines. Several faults intersect in this tectonically complex region.
articles.titleZh: 是什么让致命的委内瑞拉地震与众不同
articles.summaryZh: 委内瑞拉连续发生的两次大地震似乎可能涉及两条独立的断层线。多条断层在这个构造复杂的地区交叉。

contentParagraphs.en: People and rescuers work on the debris of a collapsed building in Caracas after a pair of strong earthquakes struck Venezuela.
contentParagraphs.zh: 委内瑞拉发生两次强烈地震后，人们和救援人员在加拉加斯一座倒塌的建筑物的碎片上工作。

contentParagraphs.en: One major earthquake striking Caracas — where older buildings are vulnerable to strong shaking — could have caused widespread damage. A pair of them less than a minute apart was uniquely catastrophic, says William Barnhart, a geophysicist at the U.S. Geological Survey in Golden, Colo.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在加拉加斯发生的一次大地震中，旧建筑物容易受到强烈震动的影响，这可能会造成广泛的破坏。位于科罗拉多州戈尔登的美国地质调查局的地球物理学家威廉·巴恩哈特（ William Barnhart ）说，两人相距不到一分钟，这是一场独特的灾难。

contentParagraphs.en: "A magnitude 7.2 earthquake alone in this region would be devastating," says Barnhart. "But it was followed 39 seconds later by a magnitude 7.5 earthquake that's about three times more powerful."
contentParagraphs.zh: Barnhart说： “仅在这个地区就发生7.2级地震将是毁灭性的。” “但39秒后发生了7.5级地震，强度约为3倍。”

contentParagraphs.en: The fact that they struck on land, close to major population centers, says Barnhart, made them especially deadly. "It's just an awful tragedy," he says.
contentParagraphs.zh: Barnhart说，他们袭击了靠近主要人口中心的陆地，这一事实使他们特别致命。“这只是一场可怕的悲剧，”他说。

contentParagraphs.en: Barnhart says it's too early to say exactly what happened under the earth, but it appears these two quakes may have occurred on two separate faults. Several faults intersect in this tectonically complex region.
contentParagraphs.zh: 巴恩哈特说，现在说到底发生了什么还为时过早，但这两次地震似乎可能是在两个不同的断层上发生的。在这个构造复杂的区域，有几个断层相交。

contentParagraphs.en: "There's not just a single easily identifiable fault that you can point to and say, 'The earthquake definitely happened on this fault,'" says Barnhart.
contentParagraphs.zh: Barnhart说： “不仅仅是一个容易识别的断层，你可以指出并说， '地震肯定发生在这个断层上。'”

contentParagraphs.en: Historically, when experts evaluate earthquake risk, they haven't necessarily accounted for this multifault scenario, says Chris Goldfinger, a paleoseismologist at Oregon State University. "We always tend to kind of assume that earthquakes will just be on one fault and only on one fault," he says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 俄勒冈州立大学的古地震学家克里斯·戈德芬格（ Chris Goldfinger ）说，从历史上看，当专家评估地震风险时，他们不一定能解释这种多故障情景。他说： “我们总是倾向于假设地震只发生在一个断层上，而且只发生在一个断层上。”

contentParagraphs.en: In 2016, a multifault event — the Kaikōura earthquake in New Zealand — took people by surprise, says Goldfinger, and changed scientists' understanding of how interconnected faults can trigger multiple ruptures. Assuming the Venezuelan quakes turn out to be similar, he says, it will be important information for those who study this kind of risk.
contentParagraphs.zh: 金手指说， 2016年，一场多断层事件--新西兰凯库拉地震--让人们感到意外，并改变了科学家对相互关联的断层如何引发多次断裂的理解。他说，假设委内瑞拉的地震结果相似，这对于研究这种风险的人来说将是重要的信息。

contentParagraphs.en: "The first one was completely out of the blue. We had no idea that could happen at all," says Goldfinger. "And here's a second one 10 years later where two really big earthquakes happened on separate faults."
contentParagraphs.zh: “第一个是完全出乎意料的。我们根本不知道会发生这种情况，” Goldfinger说。“这是10年后的第二次，两次非常大的地震发生在不同的断层上。”

contentParagraphs.en: Around the world there are other places with multiple faults. Parts of California's fault system, including the San Andreas Fault, have similarly complex tectonics.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在世界各地，还有其他地方有多个故障。加利福尼亚断层系统的部分地区，包括圣安德烈亚斯断层，也有类似的复杂构造。

contentParagraphs.en: A recent study found that parts of the San Andreas and San Jacinto fault systems may now be at their highest modeled stress levels in at least 1,000 years. Experts say that area in California is far more prepared for such an event than Venezuela.
contentParagraphs.zh: 最近的一项研究发现，圣安德烈亚斯和圣哈辛托断层系统的部分地区现在可能处于至少1000年来的最高模拟应力水平。专家表示，与委内瑞拉相比，加利福尼亚州的这一地区对这一事件的准备程度要高得多。

contentParagraphs.en: That level of preparedness is rare, says Goldfinger. In many places, engineering has not kept pace with rapidly evolving earthquake science.
contentParagraphs.zh: Goldfinger说，这种准备水平是罕见的。在许多地方，工程没有跟上快速发展的地震科学的步伐。

contentParagraphs.en: "Many of these buildings around the world were built before plate tectonics," he says. "But it's just an intimidatingly difficult thing to think about retrofitting entire cities."
contentParagraphs.zh: 他说： “世界各地的许多这些建筑都是在板块构造之前建造的。” “但考虑改造整个城市是一件非常困难的事情。”

contentParagraphs.en: The Venezuelan quakes came in quick succession with two other earthquakes elsewhere, one in Japan and one in California. " As we understand things, these earthquakes are all completely unrelated," says Barnhart, the geophysicist from USGS.
contentParagraphs.zh: 委内瑞拉地震接踵而至，其他地方也发生了两次地震，一次在日本，一次在加利福尼亚。“据我们所知，这些地震都是完全无关的，”美国地质调查局的地球物理学家巴恩哈特说。

contentParagraphs.en: He notes that while the earthquakes in Venezuela were unusual in many ways, their coincidence with two other, unrelated earthquakes on the same day was less remarkable than it might seem.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他指出，虽然委内瑞拉的地震在许多方面都不寻常，但它们与同一天发生的另外两次无关的地震并不像看起来那么引人注目。

contentParagraphs.en: " Earthquakes happen all the time," says Barnhart. "Most people don't pay attention because most of the earthquakes are out to sea and nobody is impacted."
contentParagraphs.zh: “地震总是会发生的，” Barnhart说。“大多数人都不注意，因为大多数地震都在海上，没有人受到影响。”

articles.title: A little bird told her: scientist wins $100,000 prize for decoding birdsong
articles.summary:
Julie Elie worked out how zebra finches announce who they are, what they are doing and use individual signatures
A scientist who decoded the dictionary that a bird uses to communicate has won a $100,000 prize for making progress towards a world in which humans can talk to the ani
articles.titleZh: 一只小鸟告诉她：科学家破译鸟鸣声赢得10万美元奖金
articles.summaryZh:
朱莉·埃利（Julie Elie）研究了斑胸草雀如何宣布它们是谁、它们在做什么以及如何使用个人签名
一位科学家破译了鸟类用来交流的字典，并赢得了 10 万美元的奖金，以表彰其在实现人类可以与鸟类对话的世界方面取得的进展。

contentParagraphs.en: A scientist who decoded the dictionary that a bird uses to communicate has won a $100,000 prize for making progress towards a world in which humans can talk to the animals – without being met with a blank response.Dr Julie Elie at the University of California, Berkeley, was awarded the 2026 Coller-Dolittle prize for two-way interspecies communication after working out the 11 core calls in the zebra finch vocabulary and their meanings.Her work revealed how the birds announce who they are and what they are doing, and recognise one another regardless of what they are saying by using individual signatures. She also found that at times, the birds confused calls with similar meanings more than those that sounded the same.“I’m really super-honoured,” Elie said on winning the prize, adding that she hoped the work was a step forwards in the “great endeavour” to communicate with animals. Prof Yossi Yovel, a zoologist at Tel Aviv University and chair of the panel of judges, said the work marked “a key moment in the field”.The prize was launched in 2024 by the Jeremy Coller Foundation, which promotes awareness of animal welfare and animal sentience, in partnership with Tel Aviv University. Beyond the annual prizes for progress, the foundation has established a $10m grand prize for cracking the problem of two-way human-animal communication.Elie decided to study zebra finches because they are so vocal – meaning they produce plenty of data. “The question I asked myself when hearing these chatty songbirds was what are they saying?” she said.For more than a decade, Elie observed and recorded the sounds the birds made and classified the calls according to the situation and the bird that made them. She then used machine learning to analyse what and how information was encoded in the calls. Finally, she ran tests that showed the birds agreed with her classification.In one test, zebra finches were played various calls from their repertoire when they tapped a button. Some calls were followed by a reward of some seeds. As the test went on, the birds learned to tap the button to skip unrewarding calls. It’s similar to scrolling videos on social media, Elie said, moving on when the start of the video looks dull.The birds occasionally made mistakes, but when that happened, they often confused calls that had the same meaning rather than the same sound. “Their responses indicated they have a mental imagery of the meaning of their vocalisations,” Elie said. “In other words, that they understand the meaning of their call types.”Prof Jonathan Birch, a philosopher at the London School of Economics, who was on the judging panel, said Elie had done “absolutely phenomenal work” for more than 15 years, “not just building up a dictionary of the 11 ‘core words’ of the zebra finch’s vocabulary, but also asking the finches themselves, through ingenious experimental techniques, whether she’s got the meanings right. It’s a stunning example of how to move rigorously from recording thousands of calls to understanding their meanings.”Other scientists shortlisted for the prize included a French team that showed how African striped mice reveal their identity through ultrasonic squeaks; a Swiss-US team that found bonobos combine their calls into sequences that resemble human sentences; and another French team that worked with researchers in Côte d’Ivoire to understand chimpanzee hoos and yelps.A French team that worked with researchers in Côte d’Ivoire was shortlisted for understanding the hoos and yelps of chimpanzees. Photograph: Danita Delimont/AlamyAdvances in artificial intelligence are transforming hopes that humans could one day communicate with animals in a coherent and meaningful way. Armed with machine learning algorithms, researchers are deciphering how animal calls convey meaning. But there is a long way to go to reach back and forth communication, Yovel said.Jeremy Coller, the British billionaire financier behind the prize, was more optimistic. “I’m convinced this is now inevitable,” he said. “It’s inevitable because AI is accelerating so fast. I have absolute conviction we will crack the code by 2030, a breakthrough that will benefit humans and our fellow animals the world over.”
contentParagraphs.zh: 一位科学家破译了鸟类用来交流的字典，并获得了 10 万美元的奖金，以表彰其在实现人类可以与动物对话的世界方面取得的进展，而无需得到空白回应。加州大学伯克利分校的 Julie Elie 博士在研究出斑胸草雀词汇中的 11 种核心叫声及其含义后，获得了 2026 年科勒-杜立特双向种间交流奖。她的工作揭示了鸟类如何宣布自己的身份他们是谁以及他们在做什么，并且无论他们在说什么，都可以通过使用个人签名来识别彼此。她还发现，有时鸟类对具有相似含义的叫声的混淆程度要高于那些听起来相同的叫声。“我真的非常荣幸，”埃利在获奖时说道，并补充说她希望这项工作是在与动物交流的“伟大努力”中向前迈出的一步。特拉维夫大学动物学家兼评委会主席约西·约维尔教授表示，这项工作标志着“该领域的一个关键时刻”。该奖项由杰里米·科勒基金会于 2024 年设立，该基金会旨在提高动物福利和动物感知意识，与特拉维夫大学合作。除了年度进步奖之外，该基金会还设立了 1000 万美元的大奖，用于奖励解决人与动物双向交流的问题。 Elie 决定研究斑胸草雀，因为它们的声音非常大，这意味着它们会产生大量数据。 “当听到这些健谈的鸣禽时，我问自己的问题是它们在说什么？”她说。 十多年来，埃利观察并记录了鸟类发出的声音，并根据情况和发出声音的鸟类对叫声进行了分类。然后，她使用机器学习来分析通话中信息的编码内容和方式。最后，她进行了测试，结果显示这些鸟儿同意她的分类。在一项测试中，当斑胸草雀点击按钮时，就会播放它们曲目中的各种叫声。一些呼吁之后是一些种子的奖励。随着测试的进行，鸟儿学会了点击按钮来跳过无回报的叫声。埃利说，这类似于在社交媒体上滚动视频，当视频的开头看起来很沉闷时，就会继续前进。鸟儿偶尔会犯错误，但当这种情况发生时，它们经常会混淆具有相同含义而不是相同声音的叫声。 “他们的反应表明他们对自己发声的含义有心理想象，”埃利说。 “换句话说，它们理解自己叫声类型的含义。”评审团成员之一、伦敦经济学院哲学家乔纳森·伯奇教授表示，埃利在超过 15 年的时间里做了“绝对非凡的工作”，“不仅为斑胸草雀词汇的 11 个‘核心词’建立了字典，而且还通过巧妙的实验技术询问雀类自己是否理解了这些含义。”这是一个令人惊叹的例子，展示了如何严格地从记录数千个电话到理解其含义。瑞士和美国的一个研究小组发现倭黑猩猩将它们的叫声组合成类似于人类句子的序列；另一个法国团队与科特迪瓦的研究人员合作，了解黑猩猩的叫声和叫声。一个与科特迪瓦的研究人员合作的法国团队因了解黑猩猩的叫声和叫声而入围。照片：Danita Delimont/Alamy 人工智能的进步正在改变人类有一天能够以一种连贯且有意义的方式与动物进行交流的希望。借助机器学习算法，研究人员正在破译动物叫声如何传达意义。但约维尔表示，要实现双向沟通还有很长的路要走。该奖项背后的英国亿万富翁金融家杰里米·科勒(Jeremy Coller)则更为乐观。 “我相信这现在是不可避免的，”他说。 “这是不可避免的，因为人工智能的发展速度如此之快。我绝对相信我们将在 2030 年破解密码，这一突破将使人类和全世界的动物同胞受益。”

articles.title: Asia stock markets slide as tech shares slump
articles.summary: Trading on South Korea's Kospi index was halted for the third time this week to prevent panic selling.
articles.titleZh: 随着科技股暴跌，亚洲股市下滑
articles.summaryZh: 韩国综合股价指数交易本周第三次暂停，以防止恐慌性抛售。

contentParagraphs.en: Asian stock markets fell sharply on Friday, led by a sell-off in technology firms as investors worried that recent jumps in share prices had gone too far.Trading on South Korea's Kospi was temporarily halted as an 8% fall in the benchmark index triggered a mechanism intended to curb panic selling. The index closed 5.8% lower.It comes after shares in Apple fell sharply on Thursday after it announced it would raise the prices of its iPads and MacBooks due to the soaring cost of computer chips.Some investors are also concerned about the hundreds of billions of dollars being spent this year by big tech firms to build artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure.Traders are reassessing the valuations of tech stocks, while some are taking profits after a rally in recent months, said senior partner David Makaryan from the Alpha Pacific Group, an investment firm."The long term investment case for AI remains compelling, but investors are becoming far more selective about which companies can justify the valuations the market has assigned to them," Makaryan said.Elsewhere in Asia, Japan's Nikkei 225 closed more than 4% lower as shares in technology investment giant SoftBank fell by 12.5%.Other major indexes in the region, including in Taiwan and mainland China, were also sharply lower.Share trading in South Korea has been particularly volatile in recent months.Friday's 20-minute halt on the Kospi marked the third time the so-called circuit breaker has been triggered this week and the fifth such event this year.On Thursday in the US, Apple shares dropped by 6% - its biggest one-day fall in more than a year.Microsoft shares also fell after it announced higher prices for its Xbox gaming consoles, citing higher costs of components.The moves have raised concerns that rising component prices could hit sales of devices, which in turn may slow demand for computer chips.The high cost of commercialising AI tools is gradually being passed on to consumers, said analyst Raymond Woo from Kyoto University Innovation Capital.That "naturally raises questions" about how quickly demand for such tools will match the investment into AI, and whether the valuations of tech stocks today are realistic, Woo said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 周五，亚洲股市大幅下跌，科技公司遭到抛售，投资者担心近期股价上涨幅度过大。韩国综合股价指数 (Kospi) 交易暂时停止，因为基准指数下跌 8%，触发了旨在遏制恐慌性抛售的机制。该指数收盘下跌 5.8%。此前，由于电脑芯片成本飙升，苹果公司宣布将提高 iPad 和 MacBook 的价格，周四该公司股价大幅下跌。一些投资者还对大型科技公司今年花费数千亿美元建设人工智能 (AI) 基础设施感到担忧。Alpha Pacific Group 高级合伙人 David Makaryan 表示，交易员正在重新评估科技股的估值，而一些人则在近几个月上涨后获利了结， Makaryan 表示：“人工智能的长期投资案例仍然引人注目，但投资者对哪些公司能够证明市场赋予其估值的合理性变得更加挑剔。”在亚洲其他地区，日本日经 225 指数收盘下跌超过 4%，科技投资巨头软银的股价下跌了 12.5%。该地区的其他主要指数，包括台湾和中国大陆，也大幅下跌。韩国的股票交易最近尤其波动。周五 Kospi 指数暂停 20 分钟，标志着本周第三次触发所谓的熔断机制，也是今年第五次此类事件。周四，美国苹果公司股价下跌 6%，创下一年多来最大单日跌幅。微软宣布提高 Xbox 游戏机价格后，其股价也下跌，理由是零部件成本上涨。这些举措引发了人们的担忧，即零部件价格上涨可能会影响设备销售，进而可能减缓对计算机芯片的需求。京都大学创新资本公司的分析师 Raymond Woo 表示，人工智能工具商业化的成本正在逐渐转嫁到消费者身上。Woo 表示，这“自然会引发一些问题”，即对此类工具的需求多快能与人工智能的投资相匹配，以及目前科技股的估值是否现实。

articles.title: This week in science: Exoplanetary salt clouds, ancient families and laughter's origin
articles.summary: Hosts from NPR's science podcast Short Wave talk about an exoplanet with pink, salty clouds, the surprising social structure of ancient human relatives and the origins of laughter.
articles.titleZh: 本周科学：系外行星盐云、古老家庭和笑声的起源
articles.summaryZh: NPR 科学播客短波的主持人谈论了一颗带有粉红色咸云的系外行星、古代人类亲戚令人惊讶的社会结构以及笑声的起源。

contentParagraphs.en: SCOTT DETROW, HOST: It's ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News. I'm Scott Detrow.JUANA SUMMERS, HOST: And I'm Juana Summers. And it's time now for our science news roundup from Short Wave, NPR's science podcast. I'm joined by two members of the team, Angela Zhang and Regina Barber. Hi to both of you.REGINA BARBER, BYLINE: Hey.ANGELA ZHANG, BYLINE: Hello.SUMMERS: So I know that y'all have brought us three science stories that caught your attention this week. Tell us what they are.ZHANG: The first is the weather on an exoplanet that could also be a failed star.BARBER: An intriguing find in the social structure of ancient human relatives.ZHANG: And what we can learn about ourselves from laughing primates.SUMMERS: OK, Gina (ph), I would love it if we could start in space. Tell me...BARBER: Yes.SUMMERS: ...About the weather on another planet.BARBER: Yeah. This might not even be a planet that we're talking about here. There's a debate over this object, GJ 504 b. It's roughly 25 times larger than Jupiter, and astronomers are uncertain how to define it. Is it a planet like Jupiter, or is it a failed star?SUMMERS: What do you mean by that? Like, they look the same to us from a telescope, but they're actually different things.BARBER: Yeah. Huge Jupiter-like planets and a failed star - they do look super similar, but how they get there is different. Stars need a lot of mass to ignite fusion and turn on, and without enough mass, the star doesn't turn on, basically. It failed.ZHANG: And whichever one this object is, we know that it's really dim and couldn't be fully studied from ground telescopes until a study out this month in The Astronomical Journal about GJ 504 b. Astronomers were finally able to analyze the light using the James Webb Space Telescope, and they got some information on what chemicals are in its atmosphere.SUMMERS: So what can scientists say about it?ZHANG: They could say something about the kind of clouds that were in the atmosphere. So this maybe/maybe not planet - it's about 550 degrees Fahrenheit. That's probably how hot your oven gets at home, and that might seem hot to us, but that's actually pretty cool for other worlds like this.BARBER: Yeah. And that temperature affects what kind of weather it has. Like, there are exoplanets that are so hot the clouds are made up of gemstones. Yeah, and our Jupiter is so cold that the clouds are ammonia ice and beneath that, water vapor.SUMMERS: So what are these clouds made of? And how does knowing that help our understanding of space?ZHANG: So the study modeling and this temperature all pointed towards - get this - salt clouds. And there actually aren't a lot of objects in space like this one.BARBER: Yeah. There's still a lot of questions astronomers, like the lead author Aneesh Baburaj, have. Like...ANEESH BABURAJ: We still don't know how big planets can get.BARBER: So uncovering as much as possible can help astronomers make better models for how planets and stars are born.SUMMERS: All right. Up next, we've got a story about ancient human relatives. What did scientists find out that's so interesting?BARBER: So in 2013, archaeologists discovered more than a thousand human-like fossils - so, like, bones and teeth - in the Rising Star cave system in South Africa. And one big thing jumped out. All the reconstructed skeletons looked oddly the same size, which surprised scientists because in most primate species, there's a clear difference in size between the sexes.ZHANG: And this species - called Homo naledi - they lived a few hundred thousand years ago. They walked upright, and they had human-like hands and feet, but their brains were much smaller than those of ancient humans.BARBER: Yeah. And if you look them up, they look very "Planet Of The Apes"-like.SUMMERS: OK. OK.BARBER: Anyway, a study out this week in the journal Cell may have solved this size mystery. An international team of researchers analyzed the teeth of 20 Homo naledi individuals, spanning thousands of years, to determine the sex.PALESA MADUPE: All of the Homo naledi individuals that we looked at came back missing a male marker.ZHANG: That's lead author and molecular scientist Palesa Madupe. She says the chances are about one in a million that all 20 would be female. So the team thinks this could have been an intentional, maybe even sex-specific burial practice.SUMMERS: Oh, interesting. So how big of a deal would that be if it's true?BARBER: It could be a big deal because Homo naledi isn't in our direct lineage, and burial practices are largely ascribed to humans. Although other animals do it, like elephants and naked mole-rats.SUMMERS: Interesting. So how are others in the field reacting to these findings?ZHANG: They're pretty excited. Researchers have been puzzling over this for more than a decade, actually. We talked to Charles Musiba. He's an evolutionary anthropologist at Duke University who wasn't a part of the study. Though, even with his excitement, he was also a little cautious.CHARLES MUSIBA: Could this be an intentional sort of disposal of the bodies? The answer, probably, is yes. I'd like to see a little bit more evidence, but it sort of strongly suggests that way. And the biggest question is, where are the males?SUMMERS: Where are the males, indeed.(LAUGHTER)SUMMERS: Hopefully, we'll have you on later for an answer to this next mystery. So let's move on here. For our last story, let's stay with these distant relatives. There's new research that could tell us more about how humans evolved to communicate. And you said it's from studying primates.BARBER: Yeah. So how humans evolved to communicate is not totally clear. I mean, we don't have recordings of our hominid ancestors, you know, talking. But we can study some distant relatives - the great apes. There are orangutans, gorillas, chimpanzees and bonobos. And so how they communicate and vocalize could give us clues about our own evolution.ZHANG: And what do we have in common? We play, and we laugh.CHIARA DE GREGOR

articles.title: Russia reports downing 660 Ukrainian drones, denies seeking Belarus war aid
articles.summary: The drone attack is one of Kyiv's biggest of the war, sustaining pressure on Russia as it struggles with fuel shortages.
articles.titleZh: 俄罗斯报告击落 660 架乌克兰无人机，否认寻求白俄罗斯战争援助
articles.summaryZh: 这次无人机袭击是基辅战争中规模最大的袭击之一，给正在应对燃料短缺的俄罗斯带来了持续的压力。

contentParagraphs.en: The drone attack is one of Kyiv’s biggest of the war, sustaining pressure on Russia as it struggles with fuel shortages.Russia has reported that it downed 660 drones overnight, as Ukraine maintains a barrage that is straining Russian air defences and energy infrastructure.The number of drones shot down marks one of Kyiv’s biggest launches of long-range strikes, Russia’s Ministry of Defence stated on Friday.Recommended Stories list of 3 itemslist 1 of 3Ukraine’s recovery to be deliberated in Poland amid Kyiv-Warsaw spatlist 2 of 3Top European leaders vow ‘strong’ support for Ukraine ahead of NATO summitlist 3 of 3Russia set to return to FIFA competition at inaugural U-15 World Cupend of listUkraine’s campaign of massive drone strikes inside Russia and on the Crimean peninsula has led to security fears and fuel shortages. That has stoked concern that Moscow may try to pull ally Belarus into the conflict, launched when it invaded Ukraine in February 2022.The latest wave of Ukrainian drones saw Russia intercept unmanned aircraft over 13 regions, including the capital, Moscow, and the illegally annexed Crimean peninsula, as well as the Black and Azov seas.One “massive” attack targeted the Tula region, about 180km (112 miles) south of Moscow, damaging a house and injuring a woman, said regional governor Dmitry Milyaev.Milyaev also ⁠said an industrial facility in Novomoskovsk, 200km (125 miles) south of Moscow, had been damaged.Russian media outlet Astra named the facility as the Azot plant, which has been described by Ukrainian President Volodymyr ‌Zelenskyy as critical to Russia’s production of explosives.The strikes are the latest as Ukraine continues to escalate its aerial campaign against Russia, particularly targeting energy infrastructure in an effort to cut off a key source of revenue for the Kremlin’s war effort.Its success has caused fuel shortages and disrupted Russia’s army supply lines, stalling war efforts after more than four years of fighting.With Moscow’s forces struggling to advance on the front line in eastern Ukraine, as well as to defend against the drone onslaught at home, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has accused Moscow of trying to enlist help in the war from neighbouring Belarus.On Thursday, the Ukrainian president said he had received intelligence indicating that Belarus is building infrastructure and storage bases near the Ukrainian border for military purposes.He said the measures were being taken “under obvious Russian influence, to prepare for a potential expansion of aggression against Ukraine”.“Belarus knows what steps are needed on its part for peace,” wrote Zelenskyy. “The development of border infrastructure for aggression from Belarus must be stopped.”The comments followed reports in the United States media that alleged Russia has pressured Belarus – including by leveraging key financial support – to allow its territory to be used as a launchpad for stepped-up attacks on Ukraine.Belarus denies repeat of invasion scenarioRussia denied the report the same day, with Kremlin spokesperson ‌Dmitry Peskov saying it “does not correspond to reality”.Belarus, which allowed the Russian military to assemble on its territory before crossing its southern border as part of the initial invasion of Ukraine more than four years ago, has accused Ukraine and the West of stoking tensions and attempting to draw it into the conflict.Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko on Thursday said he had warned visiting Ukrainian officials that Kyiv should not try to drag his country into war.Belarus has no intention of fighting Ukraine, he stressed, insisting he wants to reach an “agreement”.“I told them bluntly: ‘Guys, tell your president: if he thinks he can talk to us like that – and drag us into a war to boot – then he needs to understand that the nature of the war would change instantly,'” Lukashenko said in televised comments.“We received a reply: the president ‌and his team understand this. So, let’s reach an agreement, guys. We need to reach a substantive agreement,” said Lukashenko.While Lukashenko has not sent Belarusian troops ‌to fight alongside Russia, as well as allowing Moscow to use Belarus as an invasion launchpad in 2022, he has agreed to the stationing of Russian tactical nuclear missiles on Belarusian territory.Belarus also conducts frequent joint military exercises with Russia and allows Moscow to use its bases and training grounds.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这次无人机袭击是基辅战争中最大规模的袭击之一，对俄罗斯因燃料短缺而面临的持续压力。俄罗斯报告称，乌克兰一夜之间击落了 660 架无人机，而乌克兰的猛烈攻击给俄罗斯防空和能源基础设施带来了压力。俄罗斯国防部周五表示，被击落的无人机数量标志着基辅发动的最大规模的远程打击之一。 推荐故事列表 3 项列表 1 of 3 Ukraine's在基辅-华沙争吵之际，波兰将考虑恢复经济。 欧洲领导人中的三人中的三人在北约峰会前誓言“强烈”支持乌克兰。俄罗斯将在首届 U-15 世界杯上重返国际足联的比赛。 乌克兰在俄罗斯境内和克里米亚半岛发动大规模无人机袭击，引发了安全担忧和燃料短缺。这引发了人们的担忧，即莫斯科可能试图将盟友白俄罗斯拉入2022年2月入侵乌克兰时发起的冲突。乌克兰无人机最新一波攻击，俄罗斯在13个地区拦截了无人机，其中包括首都莫斯科、非法吞并的克里米亚半岛以及黑海和亚速海。一次“大规模”袭击针对莫斯科以南约180公里（112英里）的图拉地区，造成破坏地方长官德米特里·米利亚耶夫 (Dmitry Milyaev) 称，一座房屋并造成一名妇女受伤。米利亚耶夫还表示，位于莫斯科以南 200 公里（125 英里）的新莫斯科斯克的一处工业设施遭到破坏。俄罗斯媒体 Astra 将该设施命名为 Azot 工厂，乌克兰总统弗拉基米尔·泽伦斯基 (Volodymyr ‌Zelenskyy) 称该工厂对俄罗斯炸药生产至关重要。升级对俄罗斯的空中打击，特别是针对能源基础设施，以切断克里姆林宫战争的一个关键收入来源。它的成功造成了燃料短缺，扰乱了俄罗斯军队的补给线，在四年多的战斗后陷入了停滞。随着莫斯科军队在乌克兰东部的前线奋力推进，并防御国内的无人机袭击，乌克兰总统弗拉基米尔·泽连斯基指责莫斯科试图招募邻国白俄罗斯在战争中提供帮助。周四，乌克兰总统表示，他收到的情报显示，白俄罗斯正在乌克兰边境附近建设用于军事目的的基础设施和储存基地。他说，这些措施是“在俄罗斯明显的影响下采取的，为可能扩大对乌克兰的侵略做准备”。“白俄罗斯知道自己需要采取什么措施来实现和平，”泽连斯基写道。 “必须停止为白俄罗斯的侵略而开发边境基础设施。”美国媒体报道称，俄罗斯向白俄罗斯施压，包括利用关键的财政支持，以允许其领土被用作对乌克兰加强攻击的发射台。白俄罗斯否认重演入侵场景。俄罗斯当天否认了这一报道，克里姆林宫发言人德米特里·佩斯科夫表示，这“与现实不符”。白俄罗斯允许俄罗斯军方作为四年多前首次入侵乌克兰的一部分，在跨越其南部边境之前在其领土上集结，指责乌克兰和西方煽动紧张局势并试图将其拖入冲突。白俄罗斯总统亚历山大·卢卡申科周四表示，他已警告来访的乌克兰官员，基辅不应试图将其国家拖入战争。他强调，白俄罗斯无意与乌克兰作战，坚称他希望达成一项“协议”。“我直截了当地告诉他们： “伙计们，告诉你们的总统：如果他认为他可以这样与我们交谈，并将我们拖入一场战争，那么他需要明白，战争的性质会立即改变，”卢卡申科在电视评论中说道。除了派遣军队与俄罗斯并肩作战，以及允许莫斯科在2022年使用白俄罗斯作为入侵发射台之外，他还同意在白俄罗斯领土上部署俄罗斯战术核导弹。白俄罗斯还与俄罗斯频繁进行联合军事演习，并允许莫斯科使用其基地和训练场。

articles.title: Apple raises iPad and MacBook prices, blaming cost of chips amid AI boom
articles.summary:
Company says it cannot shield customers from memory and storage chip costs – and iPhone hikes could be next
Apple raised iPad and MacBook prices on Thursday, saying it could no longer shield customers from soaring memory and storage chip costs driven by the AI industry’s data cen
articles.titleZh: 苹果提高 iPad 和 MacBook 价格，归咎于 AI 繁荣期间芯片成本
articles.summaryZh:
该公司表示，它无法让客户免受内存和存储芯片成本的影响，iPhone 的涨价可能是下一个目标
苹果周四上调了 iPad 和 MacBook 的价格，称其无法再让客户免受人工智能行业数据中心推动的内存和存储芯片成本飙升的影响

contentParagraphs.en: Apple raised iPad and MacBook prices on Thursday, saying it could no longer shield customers from soaring memory and storage chip costs driven by the AI industry’s data center buildout.The move does not affect Apple’s cash cow, the iPhone. But it would take the starting price of the ⁠Neo, its lowest-priced laptop, from $599 to $699 mere months after launch.The increase shows that even the world’s most valuable consumer electronics company, with supply-chain relationships that are the envy of the industry, is not immune to a memory price surge that has dulled the outlook for smartphone and PC sales.Memory ⁠makers such as Micron have, in recent months, prioritized orders from AI chipmakers such as Nvidia. The move has helped the memory makers to record profits, but has left little supply for electronics makers. Those, in turn, have been forced to increase prices.“We have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly,” Apple said in a statement. “We have shielded our customers ⁠from these increases so far, but we have now reached a point where we need to begin raising prices on a number of products, including today’s increases for iPad and Mac.”Prices of a MacBook Air with 512 gigabytes went up $200. A MacBook Pro ‌with 1 terabyte of storage will become $300 more expensive. Apple also raised prices for both versions of its HomePod smart speaker and Apple ‌TV set-top box. Shares of the company fell nearly 5%, while rival Dell was down more than 8%.Apple’s deep supplier ties have provided some cushion, several analysts said – some of its rivals have been forced to raise prices even more sharply.Still, analysts expect prices for the iPhone to go up next.“The iPhone isn’t spared. Its hike is coming,” said Nabila Popal, a senior research director at IDC. “It was incredibly strategic for Apple to make the price hike announcements prior to the iPhone fall launch, so the headlines at launch is not the price hikes but the value the new phones bring.“Prices of dynamic random access memory, used in virtually all modern tech gadgets, rose as much as 98% in the first quarter ⁠of 2026 and are set to jump by another 58% to 63% in the current quarter, according to industry tracker TrendForce.That surge, ​dubbed by some experts as “Ram-ageddon”, has been driven by a boom in ​AI data center construction, with companies like Nvidia signing long-term deals ​with memory makers who are racing to increase capacity.Micron said on Wednesday it has locked in $22bn in such long-term commitments from customers looking to secure their ​memory supplies.The rising costs are expected to ‌weigh heavily on device sales this year, ​with research firm IDC estimating that the smartphone ​market would see its biggest-ever annual decline of nearly 14% this year, while the PC market would fall 11.3%.
contentParagraphs.zh: 苹果周四上调了 iPad 和 MacBook 的价格，称其无法再让客户免受人工智能行业数据中心扩建推动的内存和存储芯片成本飙升的影响。此举不会影响苹果的摇钱树 iPhone。但它的价格最低的笔记本电脑Neo的起始价格在推出后仅几个月就从599美元涨至699美元。这种上涨表明，即使是世界上最有价值的消费电子公司，拥有令业界羡慕的供应链关系，也不能幸免于内存价格飙升，从而削弱了智能手机和个人电脑的销售前景。近几个月来，美光等内存制造商优先考虑了英伟达等人工智能芯片制造商的订单。此举帮助内存制造商实现了创纪录的利润，但给电子产品制造商留下了很少的供应。反过来，这些公司也被迫提高价格。“我们从未见过零部件价格上涨得这么快、这么快，”苹果在一份声明中表示。 “到目前为止，我们已经让我们的客户免受这些涨价的影响，但我们现在已经到了需要开始提高多种产品价格的地步，包括今天对 iPad 和 Mac 的涨价。” 512 GB MacBook Air 的价格上涨了 200 美元。具有 1 TB 存储空间的 MacBook Pro 将贵 300 美元。苹果还提高了 HomePod 智能音箱和 Apple TV 机顶盒两个版本的价格。该公司股价下跌近 5%，而竞争对手戴尔下跌超过 8%。几位分析师表示，苹果深厚的供应商关系提供了一定的缓冲——其一些竞争对手被迫更大幅提价。不过，分析师预计接下来 iPhone 的价格将会上涨。IDC 高级研究总监 Nabila Popal 表示：“iPhone 也未能幸免。它的涨价即将到来。” “对于苹果公司来说，在 iPhone 秋季发布之前宣布涨价是非常具有战略意义的，因此发布时的头条新闻不是价格上涨，而是新手机带来的价值。”行业跟踪机构 TrendForce 的数据显示，几乎所有现代科技产品中使用的动态随机存取存储器的价格在 2026 年第一季度上涨了 98%，并且本季度将再上涨 58% 至 63%。​被一些专家称为“Ram-geddon”，是由人工智能数据中心建设热潮推动的，英伟达等公司与竞相提高产能的内存制造商签署了长期协议。美光科技周三表示，它已从寻求确保内存供应的客户那里锁定了 220 亿美元的长期承诺。成本上涨预计将严重影响今年的设备销售，研究公司 IDC 估计智能手机市场今年将出现有史以来最大的年度跌幅，接近 14%，而个人电脑市场将下降 11.3%。

articles.title: Power banks and vapes now biggest fire risk on planes
articles.summary: Lithium battery fires are the number one safety risk to aircraft, yet the number of devices found in hold bags has nearly doubled in a year.
articles.titleZh: 充电宝和电子烟现在是飞机上最大的火灾风险
articles.summaryZh: 锂电池火灾是飞机面临的头号安全风险，但托运行李中发现的设备数量在一年内几乎增加了一倍。

contentParagraphs.en: Flight passengers are being warned not to pack power banks or vapes in their hold luggage ahead of the busy summer holiday travel period beginning for parts of the UK.The fire risk posed by lithium batteries is now the number one safety risk to aircraft, according to the aviation regulator, as the number of devices found in hold bags has nearly doubled in a year.The Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) says the average person now takes four different lithium powered devices on a flight.Ahead of the school summer holidays, which begin in Scotland first this week, people are being reminded to take devices in the cabin with them.The batteries can store huge amounts of energy in a small space, and are now commonly used in lots of electrical items including laptops, vapes, power banks, mobile phones and smart watches.They're incredibly useful and versatile. But if the batteries overheat or are defective, a fire can result which spreads very quickly and is hard to control.In 2024, 316 incidents of devices with lithium batteries detected in hold bags were reported to UK authorities. In 2025, that rose to 643.Reports of devices overheating or malfunctioning also nearly doubled the same year, from 123 to 206.Most of these issues occurred in the cabin where crew could deal with the situation, but the concern is that if this happens in the hold, the problem may not be discovered until it's too late to control it.The CAA believes many passengers still aren't aware of the rules.Passengers are being reminded:To take items like mobile phones, vapes and power banks on board in the cabin.Only two power banks per person are allowed on a flight, and they can never be charged onboard.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在英国部分地区开始繁忙的暑假旅行之前，航班乘客被警告不要在托运行李中携带充电宝或电子烟。根据航空监管机构的说法，锂电池造成的火灾风险现在是飞机面临的头号安全风险，因为托运行李中发现的设备数量在一年内几乎增加了一倍。民航局 (CAA) 表示，现在平均每个人在航班上携带四种不同的锂供电设备。在本周首先在苏格兰开始的学校暑假之前，人们正在提醒您将设备带入机舱。电池可以在狭小的空间内存储大量能量，现在常用于许多电子产品，包括笔记本电脑、电子烟、移动电源、手机和智能手表。它们非常有用且用途广泛。但如果电池过热或有缺陷，可能会导致火灾，火灾蔓延速度非常快且难以控制。 2024 年，英国当局收到了 316 起在手提袋中检测到锂电池设备的事故报告。到了 2025 年，这一数字上升到 643 起。同年，有关设备过热或故障的报告也几乎翻了一番，从 123 起增至 206 起。这些问题大部分发生在机组人员可以处理情况的客舱内，但令人担忧的是，如果这种情况发生在货舱中，问题可能不会被发现，直到为时已晚，无法控制。CAA 认为，许多乘客仍然不了解这些规定。乘客被提醒：携带以下物品：机舱内可携带手机、电子烟和充电宝。航班上每人仅允许携带两个充电宝，且不得在机上充电。

contentParagraphs.en: To turn off laptops completely if they're going to be put in check in bags.Tim Alderslade, Chief Executive of Airlines UK, says the risk of lithium battery incidents was a "growing challenge" as the number of electronic devices people use increases."Whilst pilots and cabin crew are trained to deal with any situation the best outcome is always prevention, which starts when passengers pack their bags," he says.Giuseppe Capanna, a product safety engineer at the campaigning charity Electrical Safety First, said lithium batteries carry enormous amounts of energy, which is handy for recharging devices.However, it also means that when things go wrong, they can cause "ferocious" fires which are difficult to put out."When these products are packed in your baggage, there is no access to them. So if something goes wrong, they can cause a really devastating fire that can have real dangerous consequences," he said.While most devices with these batteries are safe, Capanna said it was "substandard versions" bought through third-party sellers that usually caused these problems."It is really important that we make sure that we only bring safe, tested products with us on holiday," he added.Additional reporting from Danielle Codd
contentParagraphs.zh: 如果笔记本电脑要放在托运行李中，则要完全关闭笔记本电脑。英国航空公司首席执行官蒂姆·阿尔德斯莱德表示，随着人们使用电子设备数量的增加，锂电池事故的风险是一个“日益严峻的挑战”。“虽然飞行员和机组人员接受过应对任何情况的培训，但最好的结果始终是预防，从乘客收拾行李时就开始了，”他说。慈善机构“电气安全第一”的产品安全工程师朱塞佩·卡帕纳表示，锂电池携带着巨大的能量。他说：“当这些产品装在行李中时，你无法接触到它们。因此，如果出现问题，它们可能会引发真正毁灭性的火灾，从而造成真正危险的后果。”虽然大多数配备这些电池的设备都是安全的，但卡帕纳表示，通常是通过第三方卖家购买的“不合格版本”导致了这些问题。确保我们在度假时只携带安全、经过测试的产品非常重要，”他补充道。 Danielle Codd 的补充报道

articles.title: In pictures: Britain swelters in record-breaking June heat
articles.summary: A rare red weather warning is in place as extreme temperatures continue across much of the UK.
articles.titleZh: 图片中：英国六月创纪录的高温酷暑难耐
articles.summaryZh: 由于英国大部分地区持续出现极端气温，罕见的红色天气警报已发布。

contentParagraphs.en: Image source, EPAImage caption, June temperature records set in 1976 and 1957 were brokenByHenry MooreThe UK experienced its hottest June day on record on Thursday as temperatures hit 36.4C in Somerset.It marked the second time this week the record was broken after temperatures reached 36.1C in Hampshire on Wednesday.As extreme temperatures continue to grip the country, hundreds of schools have been forced to close early, while transport has faced significant disruption, with train passengers advised to avoid all non-essential travel.With a rare red weather warning in place until Friday for the south of England, people across the UK are trying to cope with the heat in different ways. Image source, EPAImage caption, Swimming spots across the country were packed with people hoping to beat the heatImage source, EPAImage caption, The Met Office is predicting more scorching temperatures on FridayImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Many businesses have allowed employees to work from home this week due to the high temperaturesImage source, EPAImage caption, Last night, the UK endured its warmest June night on record, as provisional temperatures in Cardiff did not fall below 23.5CImage source, PAImage caption, King Charles got a helping hand to stay cool when he attended a London Climate Week receptionImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Children and elderly people are often considered the most at risk from heat-related illnesses so it’s important for them to keep coolImage source, ReutersImage caption, Pupils at a school in Essex were given ice lollies during a maths lessonImage source, PA WireImage caption, In Nottingham, a boating late was a popular choice for people trying to stay cool Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Some B&Q stores gave away 1,000 ice cream to help support trade workers on the hottest day of the year so farImage source, EPAImage caption, June marks the second consecutive month in the UK to see temperature records brokenImage source, PA MediaImage caption, A "heat-dome" settling over western Europe could bring temperatures of nearly 40C in parts of the UKImage source, BBC NewsbeatImage caption, Seventeen-year-old ice cream man Emery, from Gloucester said customers were coming out on "almost every street" because of the heatImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, High temperatures were recorded in large parts of England on Wednesday - including where this picture was taken in YorkImage source, EPAImage caption, While many people have chosen to enjoy the hot weather, health agencies have warned against spending too much time out in the sunImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Red weather warnings have been extended until 21:00 on Friday - this is the first time we have ever seen the warnings in place for three successive daysImage source, EPAImage caption, Commuters were advised to avoid all but essential travel Image source, ReutersImage caption, Schools and nurseries across the UK were closed as a result of the heatwave Image source, EPAImage caption, Beaches were a popular choice for people hoping to cool off from the heat While many people have been enjoying the warm weather, the Met Office has warned it could result in "population-wide adverse health effects", leading to serious illness or even danger to life.Doctors are reporting increased pressure and difficult conditions in hospitals, according the Royal College of Physicians.The college added there are numerous reports of older patients being brought in after collapsing because of dehydration and said its members have linked the extreme heat to causing machines and equipment to malfunction.Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Health agencies have urged people to drink lots of water as temperatures continue to riseImage source, AFPImage caption, Three women shelter from the sun as the take a punt ride in CambridgeCommuters faced long delays on Wednesday evening as public transport struggled to deal with the heat.Train operators including South Western Railway, Thameslink, Northern and Avanti West Coast have asked people to make essential journeys only and have put reduced timetables in place.Transport for London warned passengers that "very high temperatures" might cause disruption to some Tube and rail services.Image source, EPAImage caption, Transport for London has urged people carry water during the extreme heat
